summary:
René Descartes's Discourse on the Method (1637 AD) — along with his later Meditations on First Philosophy (1641) — inaugurated the modern philosophical tradition by applying systematic methodological doubt to all received knowledge and reconstructing certainty from the single indubitable foundation of 'cogito, ergo sum' ('I think, therefore I am'). Descartes, a French mathematician and philosopher who spent much of his adult life in the Netherlands, proposed that reliable knowledge required rejecting everything that could possibly be doubted — including sensory evidence, mathematical truths, and even the existence of the external world — until reaching a foundation that could not be doubted. From this starting point, he attempted to rebuild a complete philosophical system, including proofs of God's existence and the reality of the material world, though subsequent philosophers found these proofs unconvincing.

Descartes's legacy was enormous and dual. In mathematics, his invention of coordinate geometry (Cartesian coordinates) provided the bridge between algebra and geometry that enabled calculus and analytical mathematics. In philosophy, his mind-body dualism — the sharp distinction between res cogitans (thinking substance, the mind) and res extensa (extended substance, the physical world) — created the 'mind-body problem' that remains central to philosophy of mind today. His mechanistic conception of the body as a biological machine, subject to the same physical laws as other matter, was enormously influential on the development of scientific medicine. Though his specific philosophical system was quickly superseded, Descartes's insistence on clear rational foundations and systematic method defined the philosophical enterprise of the [[event:2346|Enlightenment]] and made him the 'father of modern philosophy.'
